patient.instructions:

Start by saying: “My kid keeps getting these sudden high fevers that come out of nowhere.” Volunteer: fevers spike high suddenly, last 2–3 days, then resolve; during fevers she’s cranky, very tired, won’t eat, and you notice swollen neck “glands.” If asked, add: belly pain/occasional diarrhea, painful mouth sores, sometimes a blotchy red rash on trunk/arms, and joint aches (knees/ankles). nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and provides a brief rationale (sleep initiation/maintenance difficulty with daytime impact).
Mentions CBT-I explicitly as first-line treatment and outlines at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, or sleep hygiene as adjunct).
Explains the treatment plan and includes "melatonin" explicitly, including how to take it (timing before bedtime) and a realistic expectation of benefit.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), screens for contraindications relevant to hypnotics/sedatives and melatonin use (critical; e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, severe hepatic impairment, autoimmune disease/immunosuppression, significant respiratory compromise if considering sedatives), and assesses for drug interactions (non-critical; e.g., warfarin, CNS depressants, alcohol, fluvoxamine).

Assesses for red flags and alternate causes that would change management (e.g., symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, mania/hypomania, severe depression with suicidality, substance use) and states when referral or urgent evaluation is needed.

Avoids high-risk prescribing for uncomplicated L2 insomnia (e.g., does not default to benzodiazepines; if any sedative-hypnotic is discussed, includes cautions about dependence, next-day impairment, falls risk, and short-duration use).
Provides counseling on sleep hygiene as supportive (consistent wake time, limiting caffeine/alcohol, screen reduction, bedroom association) without presenting it as a replacement for CBT-I.
Uses shared decision-making and confirms understanding, including when to follow up if symptoms persist or worsen.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, and prescribe melatonin after completing allergy/contraindication/interaction checks.
must_collect: sleep onset latency and/or nighttime awakenings
duration and frequency of insomnia symptoms
sleep schedule and sleep hygiene factors
red flags for OSA (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
RLS symptoms (urge to move legs at night)
mood symptoms (depression, anxiety) and mania screen
suicidality screen when depression is present
drug interaction risks (e.g., warfarin, fluvoxamine, CNS depressants)
steps: Elicit insomnia pattern, duration, and daytime consequences; characterize severity and precipitating/maintaining factors.
Screen for secondary causes and red flags (OSA, RLS, circadian disorder, substance/medication effects, mood disorders, mania, suicidality).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and set expectations for treatment course.
Recommend CBT-I explicitly as first-line; offer access options (digital CBT-I, therapist-based CBT-I) and summarize key components.
Before prescribing, complete safety checks: allergies (critical), contraindications (critical), and drug interaction screen (non-critical).
Prescribe melatonin with guidance on dose range and timing before bedtime; counsel on adverse effects (daytime drowsiness, vivid dreams) and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Provide brief sleep hygiene advice as adjunct and agree on a follow-up timeframe if not improving.

nl_assertions: States a primary diagnosis of Japanese encephalitis and frames it as a clinical diagnosis supported by exposure risk and neurologic findings.
Explains that Japanese encephalitis is mosquito-borne and typically acquired via mosquito exposure in endemic/rural areas (often near rice fields/pigs).
Communicates that this is a potentially life-threatening encephalitis requiring urgent evaluation/monitoring (e.g., hospital-level care) even though no specific antiviral cure exists.
Explains treatment at a high level as supportive care, including seizure control when indicated.

Avoids prescribing specific medications in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing potential meds (e.g., anticonvulsants, antipyretics), explicitly performs/requests safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical), and considers drug_interaction_check (non-critical).

Uses clear safety-net instructions: return/seek emergency care for worsening confusion, seizures, breathing difficulty, persistent vomiting, inability to maintain hydration, or decreased consciousness.

Demonstrates appropriate differential awareness (e.g., HSV encephalitis, bacterial meningitis, cerebral malaria, dengue, typhoid encephalopathy) without losing focus on JE when the scenario supports it.
Avoids misinformation: does not claim person-to-person transmission; does not claim antibiotics cure JE; does not overstate vaccine as treatment for active disease.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize encephalitis syndrome with relevant mosquito exposure and document Japanese encephalitis as the working diagnosis; provide essential explanation and urgency guidance.

Check for red flags needing urgent care (decreased consciousness, recurrent seizures, focal deficits, respiratory compromise).
Explain diagnosis: Japanese encephalitis, mosquito-borne, serious neurologic infection.
Explain treatment conceptually: supportive care and seizure control; emphasize urgent monitoring/hospital evaluation.
Record diagnosis as Japanese encephalitis.

Provide patient-centered explanation including mosquito-borne transmission.
Discuss supportive care and seizure control at a high level.
Record diagnosis as Japanese encephalitis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes immediate escalation advice (ED/hospital) and public health prevention counseling (mosquito avoidance) while remaining within diagnosis task scope.
steps: Assess symptoms and exposure consistent with encephalitis and JE risk.
Explain JE is mosquito-borne and potentially severe; advise urgent hospital evaluation.
Explain supportive care and seizure control as treatment principles.
Counsel on mosquito bite prevention for patient/family (repellent, nets, clothing) as prevention (not treatment).
Record diagnosis as Japanese encephalitis.

patient.instructions:

Volunteer that you get winded faster than before, sometimes your heart suddenly races/pounds, and you’ve had a couple moments you felt you might pass out after pushing yourself.

Do NOT volunteer that you’ve been avoiding activity out of fear, had brief gray-vision “blackout” moments, or palpitations after stress/big meals unless specifically asked about fainting, activity avoidance, or triggers.
